name: La Plata area claims and mines
short_description: High-elevation BLM rockhounding site in northern Utah's Cache County with quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Free public access with 25 lb daily collection limits.
description:
The La Plata area claims and mines lie within Cache County's productive mining district, situated on public BLM land at 7,433 feet elevation. This location offers rockhounders access to quartz-rich deposits and feldspar-laden formations typical of the Wasatch Range geological province. The gentle slopes and accessible terrain make this a practical destination for both recreational collectors and families seeking Utah rockhounding experiences.

Geologically, this area sits within the Precambrian and early Paleozoic metamorphic complex of the northern Wasatch Mountains, where ancient granite intrusives and metamorphosed sedimentary rocks host pegmatitic deposits rich in quartz, feldspar, and mica. Historical mining activity in the La Plata district targeted these mineral-rich zones, and scattered claims and old workings remain visible across the landscape, offering windows into past prospecting efforts.

Rockhounders visiting the La Plata area typically search exposed rock outcrops along hillsides and wash areas where weathering exposes fresh mineral specimens. Quartz crystals and feldspar fragments are most commonly recovered, though patient collectors may find mica sheets and occasional fine gold dust in wash sediments. The site's elevation brings cooler temperatures and manageable conditions during Utah's warmer months, though winter snow can limit access.

As BLM public land, this location welcomes non-commercial rockhounding under standard personal use regulations. The combination of scenic Cache County mountain views, accessible geology, and minimal fees makes La Plata a worthwhile addition to any Utah rockhounding itinerary.
history: The La Plata mining district reflects the Precambrian and Paleozoic geological history of the Wasatch Range, where granitic magmas intruded metamorphic basement rocks approximately 1.7 billion years ago, creating pegmatitic zones enriched with quartz, feldspar, and mica. Early 20th-century prospectors recognized the mineral potential of these formations and established claims throughout the area, leaving behind evidence of hand-digging operations and exploration tunnels that helped define the productive zones still visible today.
tips: Search along exposed hillside outcrops on the gentle slopes where weathering has broken apart feldspar-rich granite; quartz crystals often cluster near these weathered zones. Bring a small geology hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from matrix rock, and a 5-gallon bucket to stay within the 25 lb daily limit while collecting. Look for milky white to clear quartz crystals and creamy-tan feldspar fragments; mica sheets often peel away from exposed granite faces on steeper terrain higher on the slopes.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7,433 feet with gentle surrounding slopes. Mixture of exposed rocky terrain and scattered sagebrush vegetation typical of high-elevation Utah foothills. Walking distance from parking varies but typically ranges from 100 feet to 0.5 miles across moderate footing with exposed bedrock and loose talus. Terrain is relatively open with good sightlines.
best_season: June - September. Higher elevation (7,400+ ft) means winter snow closes roads and makes access difficult. Spring brings mud conditions to dirt roads. Summer and early fall offer the most reliable access, clearest skies, and comfortable hiking conditions.
written_directions: From Ogden, take US 89 north approximately 25 miles toward Logan. Turn east on State Road 39 (State Road Extension toward Logan Canyon) and continue east approximately 15 miles. Watch for BLM access roads and signs marking the La Plata area mining district. Park at designated pullouts near claim markers or historical workings. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended for rough access roads into the district.
road_conditions: Rough dirt roads with potential washboard and high-clearance requirements. Road conditions vary seasonally; impassable when snow-covered or during spring mud season.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts near claim markers and historical mining sites. Limited developed parking; expect 2-4 vehicle capacity at main access points. No amenities at site.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment use. Respect active or abandoned mine workings and do not enter unsafe structures. Pack out all trash and leave the site as you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
25 lbs per day maximum
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No heavy equipment
Respect historical mine workings
Pack out all trash
Leave site undisturbed
nearest_services: Ogden, Utah - approximately 25 miles south. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water available. Small communities near Logan (north) also offer basic services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing and exposed bedrock. No developed trails or wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Gentle slopes reduce strain but loose talus and scattered rocks require careful foot placement. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility. High-clearance vehicle access to site is challenging; hiking 0.5-1 mile from nearest reliable parking may be necessary.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding in the La Plata area near Ogden?
faq.answer: The La Plata area is known for quartz crystals, feldspar fragments, mica sheets, and occasional fine gold dust. Quartz is the most abundant find, ranging from milky white to clear specimens. Feldspar typically appears as creamy-tan to light-colored fragments embedded in granite matrix. The high-elevation location on the Wasatch Range makes these quartz-rich pegmatitic deposits the primary collectible specimens.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at La Plata area claims and mines in Cache County?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at La Plata is completely free. The site is located on BLM public land with no entrance fees or permit requirements for personal, non-commercial collecting. However, you must follow the 25 lb daily collection limit and pack out all your trash. Bring your own tools and containers.

faq.question: When is the best time to go rockhounding at La Plata in Utah?
faq.answer: June through September offers the best access to La Plata. At 7,433 feet elevation, winter snow blocks the rough mountain roads, and spring brings mud. Summer and early fall provide reliable road access, clear skies, and comfortable hiking conditions for exploring the mining district's exposed quartz and feldspar outcrops.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at La Plata?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, chisel, and small pry bar for carefully extracting specimens from granite matrix. Pack a 5-gallon bucket or collection bag to track your weight and stay within the 25 lb daily limit. Sturdy hiking boots are essential for the rocky, uneven terrain. A hand lens helps identify feldspar and mica. Sunscreen, water, and a map are also recommended.

faq.question: Is La Plata area rockhounding suitable for beginners and children?
faq.answer: Yes, La Plata is beginner-friendly for rockhounders aged 8 and up. The gentle slopes and exposed quartz are easy to spot and collect. However, the high-elevation rough terrain and remote location require physical fitness and proper footwear. Children should be supervised due to abandoned mine workings in the area. The 25 lb daily limit keeps collection manageable for families.

faq.question: How do I get to La Plata area claims from Ogden, and what vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: From Ogden, take US 89 north 25 miles, then State Road 39 east toward Logan Canyon for approximately 15 miles. A high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is recommended because the final access roads are rough dirt with washboard conditions. Avoid visiting during winter (snow) or spring (mud season). Plan for 0.5-1 mile walking to reach the best rockhounding zones.
